abstract: What does it take to build a visual reasoner that works across charts, science, spatial understanding, and open-ended tasks? The strongest vision-language models (VLMs) show such broad visual reasoning is within reach, but the recipe behind them remains unclear, locked behind proprietary reinforcement learning (RL) pipelines with non-public data. We introduce Vero, a family of fully open VLMs that matches or exceeds existing open-weight models across diverse visual reasoning tasks. We scale RL data and rewards across six broad task categories, constructing Vero-600K, a 600K-sample dataset from 59 datasets, and designing task-routed rewards that handle heterogeneous answer formats. Vero achieves state-of-the-art performance, improving over four base models by 3.6-5.3 points on average across VeroEval, our suite of 30 challenging benchmarks. Starting from Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct, Vero outperforms Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking on 23 of 30 benchmarks without additional proprietary thinking data. When trained from the same base model, Vero-600K exceeds existing RL datasets across task categories. Systematic ablations reveal that different task categories elicit qualitatively distinct reasoning patterns that transfer poorly in isolation, suggesting that broad data coverage is the primary driver of strong RL scaling. All data, code, and models are released.

citations.fullCitation: Chujie Zheng, Shixuan Liu, Mingze Li, Xiong-Hui Chen, Bowen Yu, Chang Gao, Kai Dang, Yuqiong Liu, Rui Men, An Yang, et al. Group sequence policy optimization. arXiv preprint arXiv:2507.18071, 2025a.
citations.justification: Vero's reinforcement learning (RL) algorithm is directly built upon Group Sequence Policy Optimization (GSPO), which is central to the paper's methodology. The authors state they integrate advances from GSPO and their ablations confirm its effectiveness over other RL algorithms for their multi-task setting.
citations.title: Group sequence policy optimization

citations.fullCitation: Kaichen Zhang, Keming Wu, Zuhao Yang, Bo Li, Kairui Hu, Bin Wang, Ziwei Liu, Xingxuan Li, and Lidong Bing. Openmmreasoner: Pushing the frontiers for multimodal reasoning with an open and general recipe. In CVPR, 2026.
citations.justification: This paper is cited as a key example of prior fully open reinforcement learning efforts for VLMs. The Vero authors contrast their work with OpenMMReasoner's narrow focus on visual math to motivate their own contribution of a broad, multi-domain RL recipe for general visual reasoning.
citations.title: Openmmreasoner: Pushing the frontiers for multimodal reasoning with an open and general recipe

citations.fullCitation: Haozhe Wang, Chao Qu, Zuming Huang, Wei Chu, Fangzhen Lin, and Wenhu Chen. VL-Rethinker: Incentivizing self-reflection of vision-language models with reinforcement learning. In NeurIPS, 2025.
citations.justification: VL-Rethinker is another important, fully open RL-trained VLM that the paper compares against, both in performance tables and training curves. It helps establish the landscape of existing open recipes and serves as a benchmark to demonstrate the superior generalization of Vero's multi-task approach.
citations.title: VL-Rethinker: Incentivizing self-reflection of vision-language models with reinforcement learning

citations.fullCitation: Shuai Bai, Yuxuan Cai, Ruizhe Chen, Keqin Chen, Xionghui Chen, Zesen Cheng, Lianghao Deng, Wei Ding, Chang Gao, Chunjiang Ge, et al. Qwen3-vl technical report. arXiv preprint arXiv:2511.21631, 2025a.
citations.justification: The Qwen3-VL models are the primary base models that Vero is built upon and evaluated against. This citation is crucial for understanding the starting point of the Vero models and for contextualizing the significant performance gains achieved through the paper's open RL recipe.
citations.title: Qwen3-vl technical report

intermediateReport:
## Research Paper Report: Vero: An Open RL Recipe for General Visual Reasoning

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research paper was authored by Gabriel Sarch, Linrong Cai, Qunzhong Wang, Haoyang Wu, Danqi Chen, and Zhuang Liu. All authors are affiliated with Princeton University. Gabriel Sarch and Linrong Cai are noted as Project Leads, and Zhuang Liu is the Corresponding Author.

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

The field of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) is progressing towards models capable of advanced reasoning across a wide spectrum of visual tasks, encompassing chart interpretation, scientific analysis, spatial understanding, and open-ended question answering. Reinforcement Learning (RL) has been identified as a significant factor in driving these advancements, with methods such as Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) enabling models to learn from their own generated responses via reward signals. Current state-of-the-art multimodal reasoning models, including proprietary systems like GPT-5 and open-weight models such as Qwen3-VL and Kimi K2.5, demonstrate the impact of RL in enhancing multimodal reasoning capabilities.

However, a key limitation within this landscape is the lack of transparency surrounding the development of these high-performing models. Many powerful visual reasoning models are products of proprietary RL pipelines, utilizing non-public data and undisclosed reward designs. This opacity hinders systematic scientific study, making it challenging to diagnose failure modes, attribute performance gains to specific design choices, or verify the safety and understanding of training procedures. While some open efforts exist, such as OpenMMReasoner and VL-Rethinker, they often focus on narrower subsets of visual tasks, like visual mathematics, which limits their generalizability. The application of RL across a diverse range of visual reasoning tasks presents challenges such as interference between tasks, weak transfer of learned skills, and optimization imbalances, unless data distribution and reward mechanisms are carefully structured. This research addresses these gaps by proposing and releasing a fully open, multi-domain RL recipe designed to achieve broad visual understanding.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The central objective of this research is to develop a visual reasoner that exhibits broad capabilities across various visual tasks, including interpreting charts, understanding scientific diagrams, performing spatial reasoning, and engaging in open-ended instruction following. The primary motivation stems from the current lack of transparency in the development of advanced vision-language models, particularly those leveraging reinforcement learning. Many state-of-the-art models are trained using proprietary RL pipelines, non-public datasets, and undisclosed reward designs. This proprietary nature impedes scientific progress by preventing researchers from systematically studying the factors that drive performance, diagnosing limitations, or verifying the underlying training processes. The authors contend that such hidden pipelines restrict the field's ability to achieve a comprehensive understanding of VLM capabilities and their emergent behaviors.

To address this, the specific objectives are:
1.  **Introduce Vero:** Develop and release a family of fully open-weight Vision-Language Models (VLMs) that are trained using a transparent and accessible reinforcement learning recipe.
2.  **Develop a Scalable RL Recipe:** Establish a single-stage RL training pipeline that is effective for enhancing VLM performance across diverse visual reasoning tasks without relying on proprietary data or complex multi-stage RL procedures.
3.  **Construct a Diverse Training Dataset (Vero-600K):** Curate a large-scale, high-quality training dataset comprising 600,000 samples derived from 59 distinct datasets. This dataset is specifically designed to cover six broad categories of visual reasoning tasks, ensuring comprehensive skill development.
4.  **Design Task-Routed Reward Functions:** Create a system of reward functions capable of handling the heterogeneous answer formats and diverse evaluation criteria inherent across various visual reasoning tasks, including both objective and open-ended responses.
5.  **Establish a Comprehensive Evaluation Suite (VeroEval):** Assemble a challenging evaluation suite consisting of 30 benchmarks that span all six defined task categories. This suite is intended to provide a robust measure of general visual reasoning capabilities.
6.  **Conduct Systematic Ablation Studies:** Empirically investigate the contributions of different design choices, such as dataset selection, sample filtering, data mixture strategies, and reward function design, to the overall performance and generalization abilities of the RL-trained VLMs.
7.  **Facilitate Open Research:** Publicly release all developed data, training code, and model weights to promote reproducibility, enable further scientific inquiry, and contribute to the open-source VLM community.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

The methodology focuses on developing a fully open reinforcement learning (RL) recipe for Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to achieve general visual reasoning. This involves careful data curation, a specific RL algorithm, and a multi-faceted reward system.

**Base Models and Response Structure:**
Vero models are trained on top of existing VLMs, including Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct, Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct, Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking, and MiMo-VL-7B-SFT. The models are trained to generate structured responses consisting of a reasoning trace enclosed in `<think>...</think>` tags and a final answer in `<answer>...</answer>` tags. The final answer is required to use `\boxed{}` notation for definitive, concise answers.

**RL Training Objective:**
The objective is to maximize the expected reward $R(a, y^*)$ for a generated answer `a` compared to a ground-truth `y*`, given a visual input `v` and text query `q`. The model learns a policy $\pi_{\theta}$ to generate a response $y = (z, a)$, where `z` is the reasoning content.

**Vero-600K Data Curation:**
The training dataset, Vero-600K, comprises 600,000 samples from 59 datasets, categorized into six broad task types:
1.  **STEM:** Mathematical diagrams, scientific figures, medical images (numeric/symbolic answers).
2.  **Spatial & Action:** Embodied reasoning, UI navigation, 3D spatial understanding (spatial transformations, action sequences).
3.  **Knowledge & Recognition:** VQA combining object/scene recognition with external/commonsense knowledge.
4.  **Chart & OCR:** Extracting and reasoning over structured information in documents, charts, tables, infographics.
5.  **Grounding, Counting & Search:** Spatially localizing objects, counting instances, visual search (bounding boxes, counts).
6.  **Captioning & Instruction Following:** Open-ended image description and adherence to prompt instructions.

The data curation pipeline involves multiple stages:
1.  **Dataset Sourcing and Selection:** Started with over 250 candidate datasets.
    *   **Heuristic Selection:** Discarded datasets with <1K examples, average image resolution <200K pixels, or only binary questions.
    *   **Manual Selection:** Inspected ~50 examples per dataset for correctness (<5% error), unambiguity, and verifiability. 59 datasets were retained. Some questions were rewritten for clarity.
2.  **Data Filtering (Individual Samples):**
    *   **Question Filtering:** Used Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B-Instruct to remove ambiguous, image-irrelevant, or unverifiable questions based on criteria like relevance, ambiguity, language, verifiability, and numeric precision.
    *   **Answer Filtering:** Normalized ground-truth answers using text-only Qwen3-235B-A22B-Instruct. This involved stripping units/symbols from numeric answers, converting to decimals, normalizing multiple-choice letters, and filtering out multi-value answers or complex expressions incompatible with reward functions.
3.  **Data Mixtures:** Investigated different task category weighting schemes for batch sampling (uniform, difficulty-weighted, image-area-weighted, reasoning-length-weighted). Uniform weighting across categories was adopted based on empirical performance.

**Reinforcement Learning Algorithm:**
The RL algorithm is based on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), incorporating elements from GSPO (Group Sequence Policy Optimization).
*   GSPO replaces per-token importance ratios with a sequence-level ratio computed from the average log-probability difference of a group of rollouts.
*   The objective function includes:
    *   Asymmetric clipping ( $\epsilon_{high} > \epsilon_{low}$ ) to allow less restricted updates.
    *   No KL penalty.
    *   A soft overlong penalty that linearly ramps up for responses approaching the maximum token limit.
Training typically involved 2,000 steps on 8 GPUs, using fp16 or bf16 precision depending on the base model.

**Reward Formulation:**
The total reward $R(y, y^*)$ consists of three components:
$R(y, y^*) = (1 - \alpha) R_{acc}(y, y^*) + \alpha R_{fmt}(y) + R_{overlong}(y)$, with $\alpha = 0.2$.
1.  **$R_{overlong}(y)$:** Penalizes excessively long responses linearly in a buffer zone near the `max_tokens` limit.
2.  **$R_{fmt}(y)$:** Rewards adherence to the `<think>...</think><answer>...</answer>` format with non-empty thinking content. For discrete symbolic answer types, it also requires exactly one `\boxed{...}` expression in the answer block; multiple or missing boxes incur partial or zero reward.
3.  **$R_{acc}(y, y^*)$ (Accuracy Reward):** This is task-routed, employing ten different reward functions based on the task type:
    *   **Binary Rewards:** String match, Multiple choice, Numeric (via Math-Verify with tolerance), List string match.
    *   **Graded Rewards:**
        *   **Ordering:** Full for exact order, partial for correct set with wrong order.
        *   **Web action:** Weighted match over structured JSON fields.
        *   **Grounding:** Optimal Hungarian matching of predicted and ground-truth bounding boxes (IoU/F1 with 0.5 threshold).
        *   **Clicking:** Point-in-box check for predicted click points.
        *   **Instruction Following:** Proportion of programmatic output constraints satisfied (e.g., length, format, keywords).
        *   **LLM-as-judge:** For open-ended tasks, Qwen3-32B scores responses against a reference (1-10 scale), with strict guidelines to penalize self-evaluative language and meta-commentary to prevent reward hacking.

**VeroEval Evaluation Suite:**
A comprehensive evaluation suite of 30 benchmarks was curated, spanning the six task categories. Benchmarks were selected based on difficulty, annotation quality, and intra-category diversity. Models were evaluated using the `lmms-eval` framework following official benchmark protocols, with specific decoding settings for different VLM families.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The research yielded several findings regarding the efficacy of the Vero recipe for general visual reasoning:

**State-of-the-Art Performance with an Open RL Recipe:**
*   Vero-Qwen3I-8B and Vero-Qwen3T-8B achieved average scores of 66.0 and 65.9, respectively, positioning them as top performers among the evaluated 8B-parameter VLMs across diverse visual reasoning tasks.
*   Vero-Qwen3T-8B surpassed Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking by 7.2 points on Grounding, Counting & Search and 4.2 points on Chart & OCR.
*   Compared to existing fully open RL-trained models, Vero variants consistently outperformed LLaVA-OV-1.5-RL, VL-Rethinker, and Molmo2-O-7B on a majority of overlapping benchmarks.

**Consistent Gains Across Base Models and Competitive with Proprietary Recipes:**
*   The Vero training recipe consistently improved performance across four different base models, with average gains ranging from 3.6 to 5.3 points over 30 benchmarks.
*   Vero-Qwen3I-8B outperformed Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking on 23 out of 30 benchmarks, despite the latter's use of additional proprietary long chain-of-thought data.
*   Vero-Qwen3T-8B achieved improvements over Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking on 24 out of 30 benchmarks, with notable gains in Grounding, Counting & Search (+7.2) and Captioning & Instruction Following (+1.1).
*   Vero-Qwen25-7B, starting from a weaker base model (Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct), demonstrated the ability to close a 7.8-point gap and even outperform Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct on two category averages (Chart & OCR, Knowledge & Recognition).
*   Vero-MiMo-7B, trained with the open Vero recipe, surpassed MiMo-VL-7B-RL (which used a proprietary RL recipe) on 3 out of 6 category averages, indicating the competitiveness of the open approach.

**Broad Improvements Across Domains:**
*   Unlike prior open RL efforts that often specialized in specific domains (e.g., STEM), Vero demonstrated substantial performance improvements across all six task categories. For instance, Vero-Qwen3I-8B achieved significant gains on ChartQA Pro (+15.9), MMMU-Pro Vision (+15.1), and grounding benchmarks like MMERealWorld (+10.7) and ScreenSpotPro (+6.8).

**Consistent Advantage Over Prior Open RL Datasets:**
*   During training, Vero-600K consistently led or matched other open RL datasets (ViRL-39k, OpenMMReasoner-74k, LLaVA-OV-1.5-RL-70k) across five of six categories. It showed particularly large margins in Captioning & IF and Grounding, Counting & Search.

**Ablation Studies:**
*   **Data Diversity:** A uniform mixture weighting across task categories was found to perform best overall, surpassing alternative schemes weighted by factors such as accuracy, reasoning length, or dataset size. This suggests that balanced exposure to diverse task types is crucial.
*   **Reward Design:** The multi-route reward system, which uses type-specific comparisons (e.g., exact string, numeric tolerance, LLM-judge), outperformed a simpler `math_verify` baseline, indicating that expressive, task-routed rewards are necessary for heterogeneous answer formats.
*   **RL Algorithm:** GSPO yielded higher average scores and maintained more stable entropy during training compared to GRPO and DAPO, supporting its use for multi-task RL.
*   **SFT vs. RL:** While supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on the Vero dataset showed some gains, RL training consistently produced more substantial improvements across all task categories.

**Data Diversity and Cross-Task Transfer:**
*   Training on a single task category frequently resulted in neutral or negative transfer to non-target tasks. For example, training solely on Captioning & Instruction Following often degraded performance across all other categories.
*   Conversely, training on a mixed distribution of diverse tasks (even with the same compute budget) eliminated negative cross-task transfer and achieved positive gains across all categories. Broader exposure to the mixed training data continued to yield performance improvements over longer training durations.
*   Different task categories elicited markedly different reasoning lengths, with Spatial & Action tasks generating significantly longer responses than Knowledge & Recognition tasks.

**Visual Chat Quality:**
*   Explicitly including open-ended and instruction-following tasks in the RL training, coupled with LLM-judge rewards, was essential to preserve and improve visual chat quality. Without this, models tended to collapse into producing terse, structured answers.
*   Strict judge guidelines, including automatic failure conditions for self-evaluative language, effectively mitigated reward hacking during open-ended response generation.

**Chain-of-Thought Behaviors:**
*   Each task category was observed to elicit a distinct cognitive behavioral profile. STEM tasks, for instance, showed elevated backtracking, while grounding tasks led to suppressed introspective behaviors in favor of directed visual search.
*   Analysis of recurring low-level skills further revealed that each task category cultivated a largely distinct skill repertoire, such as data-reading operations for chart tasks versus physical/game-state reasoning for spatial tasks. Knowledge skills were the least separable, often confused with grounding.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

The Vero research contributes to the field of vision-language models by providing a fully open-source reinforcement learning (RL) recipe, along with associated data and models, for training broadly capable visual reasoners. This initiative addresses the current opacity in the development of many state-of-the-art VLMs, fostering greater transparency and reproducibility in research.

The work demonstrates that a single-stage RL pipeline, when combined with a diverse and carefully curated data mixture and task-routed rewards, can achieve substantial performance improvements across a wide range of visual reasoning tasks. This finding is significant because it suggests that broad task coverage and thoughtful data design are crucial drivers of VLM generalization, potentially mitigating the challenges of interference and weak transfer often associated with multi-task RL. The empirical evidence from ablations reinforces the importance of data diversity and uniform mixture weighting over approaches prioritizing specific task characteristics like accuracy or reasoning length.

Furthermore, the detailed analysis of chain-of-thought behaviors and skill repertoires reveals that different visual task categories elicit qualitatively distinct reasoning patterns and cognitive strategies within VLMs. This suggests that visual reasoning is not a monolithic process but comprises multiple, task-dependent cognitive styles. Understanding these distinct behavioral regimes could inform the design of more effective multi-task learning paradigms and lead to VLMs that can dynamically adapt their reasoning processes to diverse problem types, drawing parallels to human cognitive flexibility.

The public release of Vero's data (Vero-600K), code, and models has the potential to accelerate future research in several ways:
*   **Reproducibility and Benchmarking:** Provides a verifiable foundation for comparing new RL algorithms, reward designs, and data curation strategies.
*   **Mechanistic Understanding:** Enables researchers to systematically study how different training components influence emergent VLM behaviors and performance across diverse tasks.
*   **Development of Generalist VLMs:** Offers a practical framework for building models that are not specialists in a narrow domain but possess broad visual reasoning capabilities, which is essential for generalist AI agents.
*   **Mitigation of Reward Hacking:** The demonstrated effectiveness of explicit judge guidelines for open-ended tasks provides a blueprint for managing potential reward hacking in complex, subjective evaluation scenarios.

While the study acknowledges limitations, such as not establishing an optimal task taxonomy or minimal transfer set, and its focus on smaller models (7B-9B parameters), its primary impact lies in demonstrating the viability and benefits of an open and transparent approach to developing frontier-level visual reasoning capabilities in VLMs. This work encourages the broader research community to prioritize transparency alongside performance, fostering a more collaborative and scientifically grounded path for future VLM development.
overview:
## Transparency in Visual Reasoning

The advancement of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) has shifted from simple image tagging to complex reasoning. Modern models are increasingly expected to interpret scientific diagrams, solve spatial puzzles, and navigate user interfaces. Reinforcement Learning (RL) has become a primary driver for these capabilities, allowing models to refine their logic by learning from the rewards associated with their own generated responses. However, as the performance of these models improves, the transparency regarding their training often decreases.

Many state-of-the-art visual reasoners rely on proprietary RL pipelines. These pipelines utilize non-public datasets, undisclosed reward structures, and complex multi-stage training procedures that are difficult for the broader research community to replicate or study systematically. This lack of openness makes it challenging to understand why a model succeeds or fails, or how specific training data affects its reasoning behavior.

![Vero Performance Overview](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04917v2/x1.png)
*Figure 1: An overview of the Vero project, showcasing the Vero-600K dataset, the VeroEval suite, and the significant performance improvements achieved by the RL recipe across various model families.*

Vero addresses this gap by providing a fully open RL recipe for general visual reasoning. By releasing the model weights, the training code, and a massive curated dataset (Vero-600K), this work establishes a transparent foundation for developing and evaluating multimodal reasoners. The central finding is that broad visual understanding can be achieved through a single-stage RL process that emphasizes data diversity and flexible, task-specific reward functions.

## Curating a Diverse Knowledge Base: Vero-600K

A significant challenge in training generalist visual reasoners is the heterogeneity of visual tasks. A model needs to excel at numeric calculations in STEM images while simultaneously maintaining the ability to describe scenes or follow complex spatial instructions. To facilitate this, the researchers developed Vero-600K, a dataset of 600,000 samples derived from 59 distinct sources.

![Vero-600K Dataset Composition](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04917v2/x2.png)
*Figure 2: The distribution of the Vero-600K dataset across six core task categories, highlighting the diversity of the 59 datasets included.*

The dataset is organized into six broad categories:
1.  **STEM**: Covers mathematical diagrams, scientific figures, and medical imagery where answers are often numeric or symbolic.
2.  **Spatial & Action**: Focuses on 3D spatial understanding, robotic action sequences, and UI navigation.
3.  **Knowledge & Recognition**: Combines visual recognition with external commonsense or factual knowledge.
4.  **Chart & OCR**: Involves extracting and reasoning over data in documents, tables, and infographics.
5.  **Grounding, Counting & Search**: Requires the model to localize objects using bounding boxes or provide precise counts.
6.  **Captioning & Instruction Following**: Evaluates the model's ability to provide open-ended descriptions and adhere to formatting constraints.

The curation process was meticulous. Starting with over 250 candidate datasets, the authors applied a multi-stage filtering pipeline. First, heuristic selection removed small or low-resolution datasets. Second, manual quality control ensured that at least 95% of the samples were correct and unambiguous. Finally, an automated filtering stage used high-capacity models to remove questions that were irrelevant to the image or unverifiable. This rigorous process ensures that the model learns from high-quality signal rather than noise or ambiguous labels.

![Task Category Samples](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04917v2/x4.png)
*Figure 3: Examples of the diverse visual reasoning tasks contained within the Vero-600K dataset, spanning from chart analysis to spatial robotics.*

## The Reinforcement Learning Recipe

The Vero models are trained using a structured response format designed to encourage chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning. Each response consists of a reasoning trace enclosed in `<think>...</think>` tags, followed by a final answer in `<answer>...</answer>` tags. For objective questions, the answer is further structured using `\boxed{}` notation.

The RL training aims to maximize the expected reward $R(a, y^*)$ for a generated answer $a$ given a ground-truth $y^*$, a visual input $v$, and a text query $q$. The model learns a policy $\pi_{\theta}$ to generate a response $y = (z, a)$, where $z$ represents the thinking process and $a$ is the final answer.

The core algorithm used is Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), with a specific enhancement called GSPO (Group Sequence Policy Optimization). Unlike standard RL methods that calculate rewards at every single step, GSPO uses sequence-level ratios to stabilize training across diverse tasks. This helps prevent the model from becoming overly sensitive to the high variance of rewards in a multi-task environment.

The total reward is calculated using a weighted combination of accuracy, formatting, and length penalties:

$$R(y, y^*) = (1 - \alpha) R_{\text{acc}}(y, y^*) + \alpha R_{\text{fmt}}(y) + R_{\text{overlong}}(y)$$

In this equation, $\alpha = 0.2$ balances formatting and accuracy. The $R_{\text{overlong}}(y)$ term acts as a soft penalty to prevent the model from generating unnecessarily long reasoning traces that consume excessive computation without improving accuracy. The formatting reward $R_{\text{fmt}}(y)$ ensures the model adheres to the structured tags, which is critical for parsing the results during automated evaluation.

## Task-Routed Reward Functions

One of the most innovative aspects of Vero is its approach to rewards. In traditional RL for LLMs (like mathematical reasoning), a single reward function (e.g., a math verifier) is often sufficient. However, for a generalist VLM, a "one-size-fits-all" reward is inadequate. How do you score a bounding box using the same logic as a scientific calculation or a creative image caption?

Vero solves this through **task-routed rewards**. The system automatically identifies the type of task and routes the model's response to one of ten specialized reward functions:

*   **Binary Rewards**: Used for string matching and multiple-choice questions.
*   **Numeric Rewards**: Employs a math verifier with a tolerance for different decimal or symbolic formats.
*   **Grounding Rewards**: Uses Hungarian matching to compare predicted bounding boxes with ground truth, scoring based on Intersection over Union (IoU).
*   **Web Action Rewards**: Checks for matches in structured JSON fields for UI navigation tasks.
*   **Instruction Following**: Scores the response based on whether it satisfied specific constraints (e.g., word count, forbidden words).
*   **LLM-as-judge**: For open-ended captioning, a high-capacity model scores the response on a $1 \to 10$ scale against a reference, following strict guidelines to prevent "reward hacking" (where the model learns to please the judge without actually being correct).

![Overview of Reward Types](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04917v2/x5.png)
*Figure 4: The task-routed reward system, demonstrating how different evaluation logic is applied to different task types, such as grounding, ordering, or open-ended descriptions.*

## Performance and Generalization

To evaluate the effectiveness of this recipe, the authors created **VeroEval**, a suite of 30 benchmarks. The results show that the Vero training recipe consistently improves performance across different base models, including Qwen2.5 and Qwen3.

A key finding was the impact of **data mixtures**. The researchers tested whether it is better to weight tasks by their difficulty, the length of their reasoning, or their image resolution. Surprisingly, a simple **uniform mixture**—where all task categories are sampled equally—performed the best. This suggests that for general visual reasoning, balanced exposure to different skill sets is more important than specialized weighting.

![Data Mixture and Transfer Matrix](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04917v2/x10.png)
*Figure 5: The transfer matrix shows that training on only one category (e.g., Chart & OCR) often leads to performance drops in others (e.g., Grounding). The "Mix Full" strategy at the far right eliminates this negative transfer, achieving gains across all categories.*

The ablation studies also highlighted a phenomenon of "negative transfer." When models were trained exclusively on one domain, such as captioning, their performance on other domains like spatial reasoning often collapsed. By mixing all tasks together during a single RL stage, Vero avoids this collapse and allows the model to develop a more robust, versatile reasoning capability.

## Analyzing the "Thinking" Process

By looking inside the `<think>` tags, the researchers discovered that different visual tasks elicit distinct "cognitive styles." Visual reasoning is not a monolithic process; rather, the model adapts its internal logic to the requirements of the task.

For instance, STEM and Chart tasks typically generate much longer reasoning traces than Knowledge or Recognition tasks. In STEM problems, the model frequently engages in "backtracking"—re-evaluating its previous steps when a contradiction is found. In contrast, Grounding tasks (finding objects) lead to reasoning traces focused on "visual search" and spatial coordinate mapping, with very little backtracking.

![Reasoning Word Lengths](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04917v2/x11.png)
*Figure 6: The average number of reasoning words generated by the model varies significantly by category, with Spatial and STEM tasks requiring much more internal "thought" than simple recognition.*

These findings suggest that "visual thinking" is highly contextual. A generalist model doesn't just learn more facts; it learns how to change its thinking strategy based on the image it sees. By providing an open recipe, Vero allows researchers to study these emergent behaviors in detail, moving beyond simple accuracy scores to a deeper understanding of how AI "perceives" and "reasons."

## Conclusion

Vero represents a significant step toward making advanced multimodal RL more accessible and scientifically rigorous. By combining a massive, high-quality dataset (Vero-600K) with a flexible, task-routed reward system, the authors demonstrate that it is possible to train general-purpose visual reasoners without relying on proprietary secrets.

The work underscores that the "recipe" for high-performance VLMs depends heavily on data diversity and the ability to reward a wide variety of behaviors—from the precision of a bounding box to the logical coherence of a scientific proof. As the field moves toward more autonomous and capable agents, open efforts like Vero provide the transparency needed to ensure these models are not just powerful, but also understood.

keyInsights: A uniform mixture weighting across diverse task categories in the training data is crucial for achieving broad general visual reasoning capabilities and preventing negative cross-task transfer.
Expressive, task-routed reward functions are necessary for effective reinforcement learning across heterogeneous answer formats in multi-domain visual reasoning tasks.
Different visual task categories elicit distinct cognitive behavioral profiles and skill repertoires within VLMs, highlighting the multi-faceted nature of visual reasoning.
originalProblem: Lack of transparency in the development of state-of-the-art vision-language models (VLMs) that leverage proprietary RL pipelines, non-public data, and undisclosed reward designs.
Challenges in applying reinforcement learning (RL) across a diverse range of visual reasoning tasks, including task interference, weak skill transfer, and optimization imbalances.
Existing open RL efforts for VLMs often specialize in narrow task subsets, limiting their generalizability across broad visual understanding tasks.
results: Vero-Qwen3I-8B and Vero-Qwen3T-8B achieved average scores of 66.0 and 65.9 on the VeroEval suite, positioning them as top performers among evaluated 8B-parameter VLMs.

The Vero training recipe consistently improved performance across four different base models, showing average gains of 3.6 to 5.3 points over 30 benchmarks, and outperformed proprietary RL-trained MiMo-VL-7B-RL on 3 out of 6 category averages.
Demonstrated substantial performance improvements across all six task categories, with notable gains on benchmarks like ChartQA Pro (+15.9), MMMU-Pro Vision (+15.1), and MMERealWorld (+10.7).
solution: Developed Vero, a fully open-weight VLM family and a transparent, single-stage RL training recipe based on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO).

Curated Vero-600K, a large-scale dataset of 600,000 samples from 59 datasets, covering six broad visual reasoning task categories (STEM, Spatial & Action, Knowledge & Recognition, Chart & OCR, Grounding, Counting & Search, Captioning & Instruction Following).

Designed a multi-route reward system with ten task-specific functions (e.g., string match, numeric tolerance, bounding box IoU, LLM-as-judge) to handle heterogeneous answer formats and evaluation criteria.
summary.summary: Princeton University researchers introduce Vero, a fully open reinforcement learning recipe and associated VLM family for general visual reasoning, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance among 8B-parameter models on a new 30-benchmark suite. The method consistently improved various base models' performance by 3.6 to 5.3 points across six diverse visual reasoning categories using a curated 600,000-sample dataset and task-routed reward functions.

title: Vero: An Open RL Recipe for General Visual Reasoning
